coredata.dc:title: The constructions of the fully flexible s-triazine-based covalent organic frameworks for adsorping iodine and fluorescence sensing p-nitrophenol and iodine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu-Xia
coredata.dc:description: The effective capture of radioactive vapor waste during fission is of great significance to the safe utilization of nuclear energy. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are a kind of novel porous crystal polymers, with highly flexible channel structure and molecular designability. COFs containing nitrogen-rich structures and flexible units can provide effective binding sites for adsorption of iodine, and are potential adsorption materials. In this study, the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were prepared by one-pot condensation using fully flexible building blocks of 2,4,6-tris(4-formylphenoxy)- 1,3,5-triazine (TPT–3–CHO) as a knot and ether-based diamines as flexible linkers. The three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs have large surface areas (1490, 1596, and 1657 m2 g−1), good crystallinity and outstanding adsorption capacitiy of volatile iodine (4.10, 4.13 and 3.40 g g−1) with excellent recyclability. As the linker lengths increase, the specific surface areas increase, but the adsorption amounts of iodine decrease. Moreover, the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs also have high sensitivities for fluorescence detection against p-nitrophenol and iodine. Their quenching constants (KSV) for p-nitrophenol are 5.59 × 104, 4.05 × 104 and 2.35 × 104 L mol−1, respectively. The fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs have larger specific surface area, high iodine adsorption, and more sensitive fluorescence sensing performance than the COFs formed by the corresponding rigid linkers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic framework
Flexible
Adsorption iodine
Fluorescence sense
P-Nitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural characterization, morphology analysis, and pore performance
Chemical and thermal stability and morphology analysis
Iodine uptake in the gas phase
The release of iodine during heating and reuses
Iodine adsorption and release in liquid phase
Sensitivity and selectivity of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs
The mechanisms of the fluorescence-sensing system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The ss 13C NMR spectra of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA.
Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) images of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA.

Experimental (blue) and predicted (red) powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs: (a) TDADE, (b) TBAPB and (c) TBPDA samples (inset: views of space-filling models along the c-axis with the layer distances). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA (adsorption–solid square, desorption–empty circle). (b) Pore size distribution of the three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs.

(a) Gravimetric iodine uptakes of TDADE, TBAPB and TBPDA as the functions of time at 77 °C and ambient pressure. Inserts: Photographs show the color changes of iodine uptake progress of TDADE, TBAPB and TBPDA. (b) Controlled release of iodine upon heating I2@TDADE, I2@TBAPB and I2@TBPDA at 398 K.
Fluorescence emission emission spectra of (a) TDADE (λex = 317 nm), (b) TBAPB (λex = 307 nm), and (c) HBPDA (λex = 316 nm) dispersed in different organic solvents.

The changes of fluorescence intensities of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs: (a) TDADE in ACN (excited at 317 nm), (b) TBAPB in DOX (excited at 307 nm), (c) TBPDA in DOX (excited at 316 nm). (d) Relative fluorescence intensity (I0/I) in dispersions upon addition of various concentrations of p-NP; Stern–Volmer plots of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs: (e) TDADE, TBAPB and TBPDA with various concentrations of p-NP.
Synthesis routes of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs.

head.ce:title: The constructions of the fully flexible s-triazine-based covalent organic frameworks for adsorping iodine and fluorescence sensing p-nitrophenol and iodine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective capture of radioactive vapor waste during fission is of great significance to the safe utilization of nuclear energy. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are a kind of novel porous crystal polymers, with highly flexible channel structure and molecular designability. COFs containing nitrogen-rich structures and flexible units can provide effective binding sites for adsorption of iodine, and are potential adsorption materials. In this study, the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were prepared by one-pot condensation using fully flexible building blocks of 2,4,6-tris(4-formylphenoxy)- 1,3,5-triazine (TPT–3–CHO) as a knot and ether-based diamines as flexible linkers. The three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs have large surface areas (1490, 1596, and 1657 m2 g−1), good crystallinity and outstanding adsorption capacitiy of volatile iodine (4.10, 4.13 and 3.40 g g−1) with excellent recyclability. As the linker lengths increase, the specific surface areas increase, but the adsorption amounts of iodine decrease. Moreover, the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs also have high sensitivities for fluorescence detection against p-nitrophenol and iodine. Their quenching constants (KSV) for p-nitrophenol are 5.59 × 104, 4.05 × 104 and 2.35 × 104 L mol−1, respectively. The fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs have larger specific surface area, high iodine adsorption, and more sensitive fluorescence sensing performance than the COFs formed by the corresponding rigid linkers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are a class of crystalline porous organic polymers (POPs) that can be synthesized by the reversible cross-linking polymerization reactions of building blocks, a knot and linkers [1–4]. Up to now, the COFs were constructed using different reversible polycondensation reactions forming imine linkages, hydrazone bonds, boron–nitrogen bonds, and boron–oxygen bonds [5]. Among which, Schiff base reaction of amines and aldehyde condensation is one of the most commonly used reactions thanks to the facile synthesis [6,7]. Most COFs are synthesized using rigid building blocks, giving regular channels and pores an unalterable sizes, but do not maximize the advantages of the various functional groups and active sites [4,8]. The recent efforts in COF synthetic chemistry are gathering in the synthesis of flexible COFs with the advantages of flexibility and adaptability, to make the best of the regular pores and uniform sites of the COFs. Some flexible COFs were synthesized by extending the rigid konts with flexible ether bonds [8–10], for example, COFs with tri(4-formylphenoxy)triazine (TPT–3–CHO) as a building block [4,8,10–14], and that with six(4-aldehydes-phenoxy) cyclic tripolyphosphonate cyanide) (NOP–6–CHO) as a building block [15–17]. These flexible COFs show outstanding performances in research fields such as heterogeneous catalysis [11,14], fluorescence detection [13,17], and iodine uptake [8,12,16,17].

TPT–3–CHO is one of the 2,4,6-triaryloxy-1,3,5-triazines, which is a typical building block for flexible COFs [2,18]. However, most linkers constituting the s-triazine-based COFs are rigid linkers [4,10,11,14,18–23], rarely flexible [2] or semi-flexible [13] linkers. The only example of triazine-based COFs constructed by fully flexible blocks is A3 + B3 [2].

The adsorption of iodine has attracted considerable interest because of the rapidly development of nuclear power and growing anxiety with the treatment of radioactive contaminations. Radioactive iodine is one of the most essential fission products of uranium and plutonium and is harmful to the ecological environments and humankind's health [24–26]. Porous materials with high porosity and quick sorption mechanics are prospective adsorbents for efficient iodine removal. Newly emerging porous polymers like metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and COFs have been investigated as adsorbents for iodine. Their structures and iodine uptake have been reviewed [1,25,27]. COFs and MOFs show outstanding application prospects in the adsorption of iodine.

Para-nitrophenol (4-nitrophenol, p-NP) is widely found in the wastewater of printing, petrochemical, textile, pharmaceutical industries and other industries [28,29]. With its high solubility and stability in water, p-NP is one of the more challenging water pollutants. Not only does p-NP cause serious ecological problems but it is also carcinogenic or genotoxic to humans and wildlife [30]. Due to its healthy and toxicological effects, a Lifetime Health Advisory (LHA) level of p-NP (60 μg L−1) in drinking water has been established [31]. Thus, it is important to accurately and ultrasensitively detect p-NP from the complex simple with exceptionally high selectivity. To date, capillary electrophoresis, immunosensors, chromatographic techniques, electrochemical methods and spectrophotometry have determined the p-NP [30,31]. However, a critical drawback of these methods is that they require sophisticated instrumentation, long-term time-consuming, and toxic reagents. Hence, it is desirable to design simple, rapid, and intuitive sensors to improve detection efficiency. The fluorescence chemosensor may be a proper choice [29]. Unfortunately, until now no reports of COFs have been for fluorescence detection of p-NP [17].

In this work, we prepare a series of fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs using both flexible TPT–3–CHO block and flexible linkers with extended ether bonds for uptaking iodine and fluorescently sensing p-NP (Scheme 1 ). The effects of the lengths of the flexible linkers on the surface area, porosity, crystallization, iodine adsorption properties, and fluorescence sensing properties are studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization, morphology analysis, and pore performance

ce:section.ce:para: The fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were prepared in one pot via the reversible Schiff base reactions with TPT–3–CHO as a knot and 4,4′-diaminodiphenyl ether (DADE), 1,4-bis(4-aminophenoxy)benzene (BAPB), and 4,4′-(1,1′-biphenyl-4,4′-diyldioxy)dianiline (BPDA) as linkers. The obtained fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were severally denoted as TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA (Scheme 1). The synthesis of TPT–3–CHO referrd to the references [11, 19, 20, 22] (Scheme S1 and Fig. S1). The three linkers originated from commercial procurement and were not further processed.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. S1) showed that the CO stretching peaks at 1701 cm−1 presented in TPT–3–CHO are absent in those fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs, while vibration peaks at about 1635 cm−1 arising from the stretching of CN bonds emerge instead due to condensation between -NH2 and –CHO (Fig. S2) [2,9]. The formation of CN bonds is also confirmed by solid-state 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 1 ), i.e., the characteristic signals due to the imine bonds (C=N) appear at 164.96, 165.04, and 164.85 ppm for TDADE, TBAPB and TBPDA [2,4,16,19]. The distinctive peaks of the carbon atoms of the s-triazine rings appear at 172.06, 173.82, and 172.73 ppm for TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA. Furthermore, elemental analyses indicate that the measured values match the theoretical ones, indicating that the reactions of the knot and linkers are complete.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and thermal stability and morphology analysis
ce:section.ce:para: These three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were insoluble in common solvents like ethanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), water, acetone, etc. These as-prepared fully flexible s-triazine-based COF samples exhibited excellent chemical stability after soaking in these solvents, boiling water, hydrochloride acid, and sodium hydroxide aqueous solution (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4). Fig. 2 showed the thermal weight analysis curves for the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs. The slight mass loss of the materials during heating to 100 °C (150, 137, and 128 °C) is mainly attributed to the evaporation of wet stored water on the surface of the materials and also in the aperture [20]. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) indicated that TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA are thermostable. In a nitrogen atmosphere, the decomposition temperatures were respective 380, 412, and 441 °C (Fig. 2). Such high chemical and thermal stabilities arise due to the network structure of their covalent cross-linking and the electron-withdrawing triazine vertices [32]. As revealed by TGA, the weight losses of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were less than 45% (56.43%. 44.91%, and 51.36%) even if the temperature rised from 25 to 800 °C (Fig. 2).

The morphologies of the powder samples of agglomerated COFs are demonstrated by SEM images at different magnification as illustrated in Fig. S5. TDADE was spherical particles with a diameter of 2.5 μm (Fig. 2a). TBAPB was a mixture of spherical and rod-like structures. TBPDA was the spongy structure formed by the accumulation of spherical particles.

The crystallinities of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were revealed with X-ray diffraction (XRD). The crystal structures for the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were simulated with the Materials Studio (MS) package to receive optimized structural parameters

Multiple sharp diffraction peaks appeared on the XRD patterns of the three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs (Fig. 3 ), which indicated that they have excellent crystallinity. The main diffraction peaks originated from the (100) facet, occurring at about 2.7°, and other visible peaks at 9.80°, 20.64°, and 22.78° were severally indexed to the (100), (220), (001), and (201) facets (Fig. 3a). The XRD patterns of TBAPB and TBPDA showed distinct diffraction peaks in 1.44/1.71, 5.88/5.53, 11.58/11.58, 19.58/20.02, 20.91/21.63, 22.60/22.78, and 28.74/29.09° (Fig. 3bc), which arise from (100), (200), (400), (001), (201), (401), and (660) facets [2,4,10,11,13,14,19,20,22]. The results simulated by MS software showed that the experimental results agree well with the simulated values of the eclipsed (AA) stacking structure of the II configuration [19,22]. The unit cell parameters yielded by Pawley refinement are a = 35.14/35.65/36.72 Å, b = 35.14/35.65/36.72 Å, c = 4.40/4.56/4.44 Å, α = β = 90°, and γ = 120°, with good consistency factors of RP = 2.29%/2.28%/2.52% and RWP = 2.95%/2.94%/3.15%, for TDADE, TBAPB and TBPDA, respectively [2,4,10,13,19,20,22].

The permanent porosities of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were revealed by the nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements at 77 K. The N2 adsorption curves of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were in accordance with the type I, being characterized by a steep nitrogen absorption in the low-pressure range (P/P0 = 0–0.01), indicating permanent microporosities (Fig. 4a) [4,12,33,34]. Based on the nitrogen sorption isotherm data between the relative pressures of 0.01 and 0.3, the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific areas of the TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA were respectively 1490, 1596 and 1657 m2 g−1 (Fig. 4a and Table S1), which are equivalent to the fully flexible TPT-COFs-1 (A3 + B3) and greater than that of the flexible s-triazine-based COFs of corresponding rigid linkers (Table S2) [33]. And as the length of the flexible linkers increase, the specific surface areas increase. In addition, the special surface areas of COFs vary significantly in different literature [4,11,12,23]. The pore volumes of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA samples were 0.677, 0.653, and 0.861 cm3 g−1, respectively. Their main pore diameters are 0.864, 0.628, and 0.886 nm, and their average pore diameters are 1.82, 1.64 and 2.08 nm (Fig. 4b and Table S1) [4,12,32–35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Iodine uptake in the gas phase
ce:section.ce:para: On account of the thermal stability and high porosity described above, we investigated iodine vapor trapping by exposing the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs' powders to I2 vapor at 350 K [12]. The fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs exhibited rapid iodine adsorption, with an almost linear increases over 48 h and then reaching saturation within 96 h (Fig. 5a). During the process of iodine capture, the color changes from tender yellow or khaki to black provided convincing evidence for iodine capture (Fig. 5a, Inserts). Remarkably, the uptake capacities of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA were respectively 4.10, 4.13, and 3.40 g g−1, which are comparable to other fully flexible COFs, flexible s-triazine-based COFs (Table S2), and are far higher than some rigid COFs (1.66–2.75 g g−1) [36] and (0.309–0.363 g g−1) POPs [37]. Both iodine adsorption dynamics and capacities of TDADE and TBAPB were almost identical, their iodine adsorption capacities (4.10 and 4.13 g g−1) were higher than that of TBPDA (3.40 g g−1). The adsorption kinetics for iodine capture by flexible s-triazine-based COFs (Fig. S6) were smaller than other materials [38], which may be due to the structural units of the fully flexible COFs can self-regulate with a small pore size. Moreover, it can be seen that the adsorption amount of iodine is affected by various factors, while the adsorption amount has no direct relationship to the specific surface area of the COFs. Meanwhile, the affinity of the skeleton for I2 may be more critical [12,34]. Based on the nitrogen-rich groups being effective adsorption sites of iodine, we suggested that triazine rings may have a greater interactions with iodine molecules than the benzene rings [34]. As the flexible linker increases, the triazine ring content decreases, so the adsorption of iodine for TBPDA is the lowest [16].

The TGA analyses in a N2 atmosphere revealed the wide mass losses of the iodine uptaked by the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs between 80 and 500 °C. Since the pristine fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs are stable below the decomposition temperature, these weight mass losses should be mainly attributed to the release of I2 from the iodine-loaded fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs (I2@ fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs) (Fig. S7) [33,35], which were thus estimated to be 3.84, 3.82, and 3.09 g g−1 relative to the mass of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA. That is to say, almost 93.72%, 92.47% and 90.82% of the absorbed iodine were released from TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA at decomposition temperature. The iodine uptake capacities derived from TGA analyses are lower than that obtained by the gravimetric method above, indicating that the iodine has strong interactions with the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs.

Based on their pore volumes together and solid iodine density (4.93 g cm−3), the theoretical adsorption capacities of iodine (4.93 g cm−3 × pore volume in cm3 g−1) of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA upon complete occupation of their pores by iodine are respectively 3.34, 3.22, and 4.24 g g−1 (Table S1). Thus, the iodine adsorption capacities of TDADE and TBAPB exceeded the theoretical maxima, while TBPDA only filled at 80.19% of its pore volume.

To explore the adsorption mechanism of iodine, XRD, UV–Vis, FT-IR, and Raman spectra were used to detect the interaction between the uptaked iodine and the electron-rich frameworks of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs. The I2@fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were tested by XRD measurements. Under saturated adsorption, the XRD patterns of I2@TDADE, I2@TBAPB, and I2@TBPDA did not emerge any characteristic peaks of crystalline iodine and the diffraction peaks are broad, indicating that the iodine has changed (Fig. S8a and S8b) [9,17].

After iodine uptake, two broad peaks appeared at ∼295 and ∼ 376 nm in the UV–Visible spectra of the I2@fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs, which can ascribe to form the charge-transfer (CT) complexes among the frameworks of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs and iodine, further confirming the strong interactions between the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs and iodine [17,39]. Furthermore, there was no any peak of iodine crystals at about 730 nm emerged in the UV–Vis spectrum of the I2@TBAPB (Fig. S9), which also showed that iodine had changed. However, the UV–Vis spectra of I2@TDADE and I2@TBPDA are high at 730 to 800 nm, suggesting the possible iodine molecules that are adsorbed in the pores.

The valence states of the iodine inside the pores of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA were revealed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S10). At adsorption balances, the peaks in the low-frequency region are mainly ones at 105/101/−, 147/144/143, and 165/167/168 cm−1, which are assigned to the syntonic modes of polyiodides anions, basically I3 − and I5 − anions [40]. The spectra results showed that I2@TDADE, I2@TBAPB, and I2@TBPDA emerged peaks at ∼165/167/168 cm−1, which were the characteristic peaks of I5 − in the V or L conformation [9,20,34,40], whereas the peaks at 105/101/− and 147/144/143 cm−1 are respectively as a result of the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of I3 − [34,40]. The disappearance of the characteristic peaks peaks of I2 (∼180 or 213 cm−1) indicated that all I2 have been translated to polyiodide (I3 −, I5 −) in I2@TBAPB (Fig. S10c) [34]. The appearances of the characteristic peaks of I2 (∼213 or 180 cm−1) in I2@TDADE and I2@TBPDA, indicating that iodine molecules do not all converted to polyiodide ions (Fig. S10a and S10d) [19].

The frameworks have numerous aromatic ring structures that can undergo iodine capture, which can be demonstrated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S11 and Table S3). The characteristic peak positions of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs changed obviously before and after capturing. For instance, the peaks of the carbon nitrogen double bonds (C=N) of Schiff's base (from 1635 cm−1 to 1621 cm−1), the peaks of the CN of triazine (from 1357/1357/1364 cm−1 to 1400 cm−1), the carbon‑carbon double bonds (C=C) on the benzene ring (from 1600 and 1493/1486/1493 cm−1 to disappearing), the CN on triazine (from 1571 cm−1 to disappearing), and C–O–C (from 1201/1201/1207 and 1158 cm−1 to disappearing). These spectral changes suggested that the adsorption of iodine can occur synchronously the on imine bonds, the triazine rings, the –C–O–C– linkage, and the benzene rings in the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs (Fig. S11 and Table S3) [9]. This is a remarkable feature compared to most adsorbents that lack the interactions with iodine [12,16,17,34,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The release of iodine during heating and reuses
ce:section.ce:para: The iodine encapsulated in the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can be released by heating the iodine-loaded fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs at 125 °C [22]. The iodine release rates are incredibly rapid in 0–30 min (Fig. 5b). Then the iodine release rate gradually flattened out and finally balanced, which can make the iodine release efficiencies of TDADE, TBAPB and TBPDA were calculated by analyzing weight changes reach 99.60%, 99.95%, and 99.96%, respectively. And almost all of the iodine was released at this time, suggesting that the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs are ready to recover and reuse [19].

The regenerated fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can be reused to uptake iodine by exposure to iodine vapor. Their iodine uptakes maintained at 98.15%, 96.14% and 99.76% of their initial capacities after the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were used five times (Fig. S12), and the cycling percentages of iodine adsorption are 69.90%, 89.52% and 91.26%, respectively, suggesting excellent recyclability and stability of TDADE, TBAPB and TBPDA [16,19,33,35] (Fig. S13). After five cycles, the desorption efficiency gradually increased from TDADE to pass through TBAPB to TBPDA, possibly be due to the reduction of the triazine ring content and the reduced point of action.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Iodine adsorption and release in liquid phase
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the iodine captures of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were further investigated by immersing fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs (30 mg) in iodine-cyclohexane solution (100 mg L−1, 5.0 mL). When the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were put into a cyclohexane solution of iodine (100 mg L−1), the purple solutions did not fade except for TDADE fading slightly, which indicated that the iodine was difficult to be encapsulated in the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs. The iodine capture capabilities of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA in cyclohexane were estimated to be 73.68%, 41.65%, and 30.50% (Fig. S14-S15). The iodine uptake liquid phase was lower than in the gaseous state, probably due to the coadsorption of the solvent molecules. In particular, there is iodine solution rather than pure iodine filled the inner cavities of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs, which greatly reduces their iodine trapping capacity [33].

The adsorptive iodine is easily released from the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs by soaking the I2@fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs in alcohol. Over time, the color of the alcohol solution became from colorless to yellow-brown, suggesting that the iodine was being separated from the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs (Fig. S16-S17) [12,33–35]. Iodine delivery was recorded with UV–Vis spectroscopy, with 99.99%, 45.88%, and 99.97% of iodine rapidly released in 2 h, showing good disattached ability (Fig. S16-S17).

Fig. S18 represented the UV–Vis spectra of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA, which showed absorbance maxima at 379, 384, and 378 nm, respectively. Compared with building blocks, the absorption bands of TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA are the obvious red-shifts, which meant that the π-conjugated structures were extended in the skeletons of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs (Fig. S18) [41]. These redshifts observed for the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs indicated that the –CH=N– bond connects the vertices and edges as a π-conjugated, which enables for an extended π delocalization on the two-dimensional skeletons [42,43]. The maximum absorption wavelength of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs shifted small compared to TPT–3–CHO, probably due to the poor conjugation of the flexible linkers.

The solid-state electronic excitation and fluorescence spectra of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs and respective building blocks have been studied at room temperature (Fig. S19 and Table S4). The three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs generated emission peaks upon excitation with a certain wavelength. For instance, the maximum emission peak of TDADE was 421 nm at 342 nm excitation, the maximum emission peak of TBAPB was 485 nm at 361 nm excitation. In contrast, TPBDA exhibited a strong emission at 460 nm when excited at 360 nm. The maximum fluorescence emission peaks of the three flexible s-triazine-based COFs showed varying degrees red shifts as compared to the respective building blocks. Their fluorescence intensities enhanced differently due to the increase of the π- conjugated length caused by the condensation reactions, and the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs are more stable, reducing the energy consumed by vibration, just as the aggregation-induced effect (AIE) [43,44].

Interestingly, the three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can be easily dispersed in some solvents, for instance, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), THF, acetonitrile (ACN), ethanol (EtOH), chloroform, 1,4-dioxane (DOX), and acetone. The fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength of fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs were dependent on the dispersed solvents. ACN-dispersed TDADE, DOX-dispersed TBAPB and HBPDA showed the highest fluorescence intensity among the dispersions (Fig. 6 ). The fluorescence of the three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can be due to the restricted the rotations of the intramolecular bonds [45]. We believe the fluorescence of the three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs most likely originated from the isolated conjugated units in the 3D networks [46].

Fig. S20 showed that the fluorescence decreased instantly once adding p-NP into the dispersions of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs. The fluorescence intensity instantaneously reached equilibrium and remained constant. The response time of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs was below 20 s. The fast sensing process enables quickly detecting in a real-world analysis system [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs
ce:section.ce:para: Because of their good porosity and excellent fluorescence properties, the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can be used as chemical sensors for the following different nitro-aromatic compounds (NACs) and I2. A variety of NACs were selected as the analytes for the study of chemical sensing, which included 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), 2-nitrophenol (o-NP), 3-nitrophenol (m-NP), nitrobenzene (NB), p-NP, 4-nitrotoluene (p-NT), m-dinitrobenzene (m-DNB), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), and p-dinitrobenzene ( p -DNB). By adding p-NP into the ACN or DOX dispersions of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs, the emission intensity was significantly quenched (Fig. 7 and Fig. S21) [48]. For the rest NACs, the quenching efficiency was very low, suggesting that the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs showed an good selectivity for p-NP. To quantitatively investigate the quenching efficiencies of the various analytes, we employed the standard linear curve-fitting in Stern–Volmer equations, (I0/I) = KSV[A] + 1. In the formula, I0 and I indicate the fluorescence intensity in the absence of and in the addition of the analyte, [A] represent the concentration of the analyte, and KSV is quenching constant [49,50]. The KSV values were respectively 5.59 × 104, 4.05 × 104, and 2.35 × 104 L mol−1 upon the addition of p-NP (Fig. 7 and Table S5), which is moderate level as compared to other chemical sensors of NACs. Moreover, the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs exhibited the limits of detection (LODs) of 2.68 × 10−11, 3.70 × 10−11, and 6.38 × 10−11 mol L−1, which indicates that the sensitivities decrease with increasing linker lengths. These low LODs indicated that the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can be used as sensitive fluorescent sensors to detect p-NP. Similarly, TDADE, TBAPB, and TBPDA can also detect iodine with the KSV values of 1.80 × 104, 1.74 × 103, and 5.16 × 103 L mol−1, respectively. The LODs were 8.33 × 10−11, 8.62 × 10−10, and 2.91 × 10−10 mol L −1, respectively (Fig. S22 and Table S5- S7).

The competitive compounds of p-NP were selected to explore the selectivity of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs and further evaluated the ability of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs to p-NP. As shown in Fig. S23, the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs exhibited a pronounced fluorescence response to p-NP compared to the other competitive NACs. However, by using the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs, the nine NACs showed no significant changes in the fluorescence response to p-NP. All the analogous except for hydroxy NACs had minimal effects [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanisms of the fluorescence-sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: This result may put down to the electron charge transfer between p-NP and the π-electronic cloud of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs. The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs and NACs were calculated to explain the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by NACs (Fig. S24-S25). The LUMOs energy levels of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs are higher than those of NACs, providing a driving force of the transfer of electrons from the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs to electron-deficient NACs, thus causing quenching the fluorescence [17,51]. Moreover, the SV plots of p-NP emissions deviated upwards linearity at high concentrations, which could be a testimony of the energy transfer process. There are some spectral overlaps between the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs and p-NP (Fig. S26), which further demonstrated the probability of energy transfer; therefore, improve the quenching efficiency [52]. Furthermore, the presence of phenol groups in the NACs is important because the hydroxys can form hydrogen-bonds with the open nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the pore walls [43].

The photostability of the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs was carried out to investigate the reusability for the detection of p-NP. The fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs could be regenerated and reused for at least five cycles by centrifuging the dispersion in ACN or DOX (Fig. S27). Since the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs stabilize the network structures and are easily separated from NACs, regeneration does not reduce the fluorescence intensities, which make the fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs possessing high photostability of in fluorescence detection tests [53,54].

In conclusion, three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs built up with fully flexible building blocks were synthesized by using aldimine condensation of the Schiff base chemistry strategy. These fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs have high crystallinity, large surface areas and excellent porous characters, with high adsorption capacities to volatile iodine. The three fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can also fluorescent-sense p-nitrophenol and iodine, with high sensitivity and low limits of detection. It could be anticipated that the introduction of flexible building blocks into COF frameworks can greatly expand the diversity of new structures and functions of the COF-based materials. These fully flexible s-triazine-based COFs can self-regulate their pore structures and optimize the action sites, thus increasing the amount of iodine adsorption, and increasing the sensitivity of fluorescence sensing to p-nitrophenol and iodine.
Yu-Xia Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
